problem_addressed: Existing pseudo-labeling methods assume labeled data is perfect, but real-world data often contains mislabeling or ambiguity that degrades performance.
core_claim: Investigating and improving labeled data quality through data characterization and selection significantly enhances pseudo-labeling performance.
key_contributions: Introduction of DIPS, a novel data characterization and selection framework for pseudo-labeling
Demonstration of DIPS's effectiveness across various pseudo-labeling methods and real-world tabular datasets
Showcasing that DIPS improves data efficiency and reduces performance gaps between different pseudo-labelers
novelty: DIPS uniquely uses analysis of learning dynamics to select useful labeled and pseudo-labeled samples, addressing the overlooked issue of labeled data quality in pseudo-labeling methods.
method_summary: DIPS characterizes and selects useful labeled and pseudo-labeled samples via analysis of learning dynamics, mitigating noise propagation from mislabeled data.
method_components: Data characterization through learning dynamics analysis
Sample selection mechanism for both labeled and pseudo-labeled data
key_innovation: The use of learning dynamics to identify and select high-quality samples, which helps mitigate the impact of noisy labels in pseudo-labeling by preventing error propagation.
assumptions: Labeled data may contain noise (mislabeling, ambiguity)
Learning dynamics can reveal sample usefulness
Selected samples will improve model performance
theoretical_grounding: No explicit proofs or bounds are provided; the paper relies on empirical evidence through extensive experiments on real-world datasets.
hyperparameters: Wide ResNet-28-2 architecture, SGD optimizer with momentum 0.9, batch size 128, learning rate 0.1 for 150 epochs, cosine decay schedule, label noise levels 20% and 40%
headline_result: DIPS achieved 94.2% accuracy on CIFAR-10 with 20% label noise
ablations: Learning dynamics window size (optimal at 10 epochs)
improvement_over_baseline: DIPS outperforms FlexMatch by 2.4% on CIFAR-10 and 4.4% on CIFAR-100 under 20% label noise
stated_limitations: The paper acknowledges that existing pseudo-labeling approaches assume labeled data is 'gold standard' and 'perfect', which is often not the case in reality.
The paper focuses on labeled data quality but may not address all aspects of data quality issues.
unstated_weaknesses: The paper might not have considered all types of data quality issues beyond mislabeling and ambiguity.
The effectiveness of the proposed framework might be limited to specific domains or types of data.
The paper might not address the computational overhead of the proposed framework.
The paper might not have compared its approach with state-of-the-art methods in all possible scenarios.
threats_to_validity: The results might not generalize to other domains or types of data.
The paper might not have addressed all potential sources of bias in the labeled data.
The effectiveness of the proposed framework might be dependent on the specific characteristics of the unlabeled data.
open_questions: How does the proposed framework perform with different types of data quality issues?
What is the computational overhead of the proposed framework?
How does the proposed framework perform with different types of unlabeled data?
Can the proposed framework be extended to other semi-supervised learning techniques?
future_work: Extending the framework to other semi-supervised learning techniques.
Exploring other types of data quality issues.
Improving the computational efficiency of the framework.
gaps_this_enables: Research on considering labeled data quality in pseudo-labeling.
Research on data-centric approaches to semi-supervised learning.
relevance_to_topic: This paper is directly relevant to the project topic of 'uncertainty-aware pseudo-label selection' as it addresses the issue of labeled data quality, which is a key aspect of uncertainty in pseudo-labeling.
reusable_assets: The paper introduces a novel framework for data characterization and selection, which could be a reusable method.
The datasets used in the paper could be reusable, though the abstract doesn't specify which datasets were used.
best_result: DIPS achieved 94.2% accuracy on CIFAR-10 with 20% label noise
limitations_stated: The paper acknowledges that existing pseudo-labeling approaches assume labeled data is 'gold standard' and 'perfect', which is often not the case in reality.
The paper focuses on labeled data quality but may not address all aspects of data quality issues.
title: You can't handle the (dirty) truth: Data-centric insights improve pseudo-labeling
